query: association between adult body circumferences and height

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Measurement of Growth
retrieved_docs.content: The measure of adiposity: Body mass index (BMI) is a useful predictor of adiposity. BMI is calculated using weight (kg) / height (m) 2 . BMI is the single best indicator for detecting overweight or obesity. < 5th percentile - underweight 5th to 84th percentile - normal 85th to 95th percentile - overweight 95th to 98th percentile - obesity More than 99th percentile - severe obesity The weight-to-length ratio is an alternative for body mass index in predicting adiposity in less than 2 years. Self-assessment of the hip-to-waist ratio can help to guide the measure of central adiposity, Triceps and subscapular skinfolds can also be a useful measure of adiposity. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Measurement of Growth
retrieved_docs.content: Anthropometry is the gold standard by which clinicians can assess nutritional status. The major anthropometric measurements for ages up to 2 years are weight, length, weight for length, and head circumference. The major measurements for children above 2 years are weight, height, body mass index (BMI), and head circumference for the 2-3 years age group.

retrieved_docs.title: Anthropometric Measurement -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The CHDP Program Health Assessment Guidelines (guideline #4) recommend anthropometric measurements in all children and adolescents at each preventive visit to ensure adequate growth patterns and to assess the risk of obesity. Accurate serial measurements over time are the most important aspect of anthropometry. Solitary deviations from a growth pattern curve can be a normal variant or due to an acute illness. However, according to the CHDP guidelines, steady change of the growth curve on serial measurements is a reliable indicator of an abnormal growth pattern and warrants further workup. For infants and toddlers under 2 years of age, weight, length, and head circumference are indicated by anthropometric measurements at each well-visit. Indicated measurements include weight and length for children over 2 years of age. BMI measurement is recommended for all children 2 years and older to determine adequate nutritional status and risk of obesity. These measurements should be plotted on World Health Organization (WHO) charts or the CDC charts, which are gender and age-specific to compare the child to the average population. In adults, anthropometric measurements are recommended at each well-visit to determine nutritional status and the risk of future disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Mass Index -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with abdominal (visceral) obesity are at a greater risk of acquiring multiple pathological conditions and have a higher morbidity and mortality rate. [3] However, BMI has no way to account for this variable. In the calculation of BMI, height is squared to reduce the contribution of leg length in taller people, as most body mass remains within the trunk. Of concern is that with this normalization, the equation distributes equal mass to each height level, which subtracts from the utility of BMI in studies of differing body types. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Nutritional Assessment -- Function -- Anthropometric Measurements
retrieved_docs.content: Height, weight, and BMI: Measure the weight and height of the patient, as mentioned above. Patients should be advised to avoid wearing heavy garments or shoes while these measurements are taken. Bed or chair scales may be needed if patients are not ambulatory or cannot stand. In pediatric age groups, these parameters are plotted on growth charts to assess growth and nutritional status. BMI (weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared) is also calculated using these parameters, and the state of nutrition can be assessed. In adults, BMI < 18.5 kg/m^2: underweight; BMI = 18.5 to 24.9 kg/m^2: within normal range; BMI > 24.9 to 29.9 kg/m^2: overweight; and BMI ≥ 30 kg/m^2: obesity. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Clinical Significance -- Growth Charts
retrieved_docs.content: When using the WHO charts, the prevalence of short stature and obesity is similar to the CDC charts, but the underweight prevalence was lower than the CDC charts. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned, 2.3% of individuals in any population are considered short when the entire population is taken into account. However, this percentage varies across studies depending on cutoff points, study population, and geographic region.

retrieved_docs.title: Height Assessment -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The Child Health and Disability Prevention (CHDP) Program Health Assessment Guidelines (guideline #4) recommend anthropometric measurements in all children and adolescents at each preventive visit to ensure adequate growth patterns and to assess the risk of obesity. The obtained measurement should be plotted on World Health Organization (WHO) charts or the CDC charts, which are gender and age-specific, to compare the child to the average population. Accurate serial measurements over time are the most important aspect of anthropometry. Solitary deviations from a growth pattern curve can be a normal variant or due to an acute illness. However, according to the CHDP guidelines, a steady change of the growth curve on serial measurements is a reliable indicator of an abnormal growth pattern and warrants further workup. [1] In adults, anthropometric measurements are recommended at each well-visit to determine nutritional status and the risk of future disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Mass Index -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: It is also essential to understand that BMI has limited value in evaluating bodyweight health in people of short stature and does not account for differences in body types between men and women. One study of 13,601 subjects found that BMI-defined obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m 2 ) was present in 31% of women and 21% of men, while body fat-defined obesity was found in 62% of women and 50% of men. This study showed that BMI-defined obesity is highly specific (99% for women and 95% for men) but shows poor sensitivity (49% for women and 36% for men). Simply put, this study demonstrated that BMI is beneficial when determining a patient to be obese but can give false data when determining a patient not to be obese. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Causes of Obesity and Comprehensive Diagnostic Evaluation -- Function -- Waist Circumference Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Asian-specific criteria adopt lower thresholds due to a greater predisposition to visceral adiposity at lower BMI values. In Asian populations, increased metabolic risk occurs at a lower waist circumference, 90 cm (35.4 inches) in men and 80 cm (31.5 inches) in women. This population experiences an earlier onset of metabolic disorders, including diabetes and cardiovascular disease, due to differing patterns of fat accumulation and heightened metabolic sensitivity at lower anthropometric measurements. Please refer to StatPearls' companion resources, " Anthropometric Measurement " and " Physiology, Body Mass Index, " for additional information.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Mass Index -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Other anthropometric assessments require measurements of an individual's body. These numbers are less reliable than BMI because they can change depending on the measurement's location. BMI relies on the scale's accuracy used to weigh an individual and can be comparatively referred to as an objective evaluation. For these reasons, a quotient developed in the mid-1800s is widely utilized in the 21st century. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Mass Index -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Many studies have also shown a link between increased BMI and insulin resistance. One such study was performed by Preethi et al on healthy men aged 18 to 25, titled Body Mass Index a Better Predictor of Insulin Resistance than Waist Circumference in Normoglycemics . The participant's BMIs and waist-hip circumference ratios were calculated, after which they underwent a 2-hour glucose test. As the study title indicates, BMI had a statistically significant higher correlation with insulin resistance. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Height Assessment -- Clinical Significance -- Anthropological
retrieved_docs.content: The significance of measuring the height of individuals for an anthropological setting comes into play while studying the physical growth patterns and secular trends across regions and population groups. [29] We cannot apply established data for a particular population to other populations and hope to expect accurate results. Population-specific height assessments can help identify areas of concern and target populations. In the context of an overall increase in average heights worldwide, a decline in the average height of adults in a specific population warrants inquiry into factors that may be causing this trend. Evaluating average height in India over two decades, a study underlined the need to examine the non-genetic factors leading to a trend toward the decreased height of the population. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Anthropometric Measurement -- Technique or Treatment -- BMI
retrieved_docs.content: In adults, BMI is used to diagnose obesity as it correlates with body fat. However, it does not directly measure body fat and has limitations when used in isolation. Percent body fat varies with age, gender, and ethnicity. Percent body fat increases with age even if the weight stays the same, making it a less accurate measure of obesity in adults. Also, in athletes, increased muscle mass for a given height and age increases their BMI, even though they have a very low percentage of total body fat.

retrieved_docs.title: Anthropometric Measurement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The lengths of extremities are related to chronic diseases as well. A literature review shows that those with a shorter upper leg length (ULL) have a higher prevalence of metabolic syndrome. [14] Similarly, shorter upper arm length has an association with a higher prevalence of diabetes in Japanese Americans. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Height Assessment -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organisation (WHO) growth standards can be used to monitor the physical growth and development of children and infants. It estimates the percentile and z-scores for length/height for age, weight for age, weight for length, weight for height, and BMI for age. The standards depict normal early childhood growth under optimal conditions. [1] These various parameters listed under the growth chart can be used to diagnose growth disorders or address the nutritional needs of children regardless of their ethnicity, socioeconomic status, and type of feeding. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with a threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر). This interrelationship indicates that infants born preterm with low birth weight are more likely to develop reduced height, and that reduced height itself may signal an increased risk of threatened premature labor. Recognizing these connections can help clinicians monitor growth patterns and anticipate obstetric complications, thereby improving patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية (Cesarean delivery) and with قصر القامة (short stature). In addition, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة. These relationships indicate that short stature is linked both to infants born preterm with low birth weight and to the condition of threatened preterm labor, while preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are more likely to be delivered by Cesarean section. Recognizing these interconnections can help clinicians anticipate delivery method needs and monitor growth outcomes in infants at risk for or experiencing preterm birth complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1979
retrieved_docs.content: Early or threatened labor is classified under the Arabic term “تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر”. Clinical observations indicate that short stature (قصر القامة) is associated with this condition, suggesting that patients with reduced height may have an increased risk of experiencing early or threatened labor. This relationship underscores the importance of evaluating stature when assessing obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature, in turn, is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). وفاة حديث الولادة is identified as a concept within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology, indicating that neonatal death is recognized as a specific adverse event in pediatric care. Together, these relationships suggest that infants born preterm with low birth weight may develop short stature, which is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death, a critical adverse outcome captured by pediatric safety terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with both obesity (الرَبْو) and short stature (قصر القامة). Obesity shares clinical similarity with asthma and is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة). Environmental exposure to air pollution (znečištění ovzduší) is related to obesity, and tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is also clinically associated with obesity. Short stature is likewise associated with neonatal death. These interconnections illustrate how low birth weight, growth abnormalities, respiratory conditions, environmental factors, and lifestyle behaviors converge to affect infant mortality and long‑term health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ aggregates related disorders such as انسداد مَريئِيّ, تسرب المفاغرة المريئية, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة حادّة مَصْحُوبة بنَزِف, and نَخَرٌ مَريئِيٌّ, providing a unified risk‑assessment framework. Head circumference (حج القحف) shows a clinical association with a positive tuberculin test, suggesting that altered cranial size may prompt tuberculin testing in suspected inflammatory or infectious conditions. داء المتشاخسات corresponds to non‑infectious gastroenteritis, while حُمامَى denotes other inflammatory skin conditions. Collectively, these interrelated classifications depict a structured network that integrates prenatal risk factors (short gestation, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, alcohol exposure), perinatal growth and nutritional disorders, a broad spectrum of neonatal pathologies (traumatic, infectious, metabolic, developmental, hemorrhagic, and respiratory), obstructive urologic and gastrointestinal conditions, and infectious‑disease screening. The network is encoded
